items.q: What does utilitarianism say is right action?
items.correct: The action that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number — judged by consequences, not intentions or rules.
items.misconception: Whatever makes you personally happy is the right thing to do.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism says you should always tell the truth.
items.why: Students reduce utilitarianism to egoism. It is impartial — your own happiness counts no more than anyone else's. Personal happiness alone misses the aggregative core.

items.q: What does Kant's categorical imperative require?
items.correct: Acting only on maxims you could will to be universal laws, and treating people as ends in themselves, never merely as means.
items.misconception: Treat others how you would like to be treated.
items.random_wrong: Do whatever your conscience tells you to do.
items.why: The Golden Rule depends on your personal preferences (Kant explicitly rejected it). The categorical imperative tests universalisability — whether the maxim can coherently be a law for everyone.

items.q: What is the focus of virtue ethics?
items.correct: On character and habits — becoming the kind of person who acts well, aiming at eudaimonia (flourishing) through practising virtues that lie between extremes.
items.misconception: Virtue ethics tells you which specific actions are right and wrong.
items.random_wrong: Virtue ethics is about following the Ten Commandments.
items.why: Virtue ethics asks 'what kind of person should I be?' rather than 'what should I do?'. Students try to extract action-rules from it, missing its character-based agent-focused nature.
The answer examiners want is: Aquinas. “Anselm” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Hume. “Descartes” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many of Aristotle's causes are there?
items.why: The real figure is 4 causes. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
